evals.name: price-pushback-no-default-discount
evals.prompt: I'm an AE selling a $180k ACV observability platform to a 1500-person fintech. We've had 3 calls. Technical eval is going well. The CIO just emailed me: 'Love the product. Can we get to $120k? We're 30% over budget for tooling this year.' Draft my email response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: offer a discount around $150k. Skill: don't reply with a number; diagnose budget claim with calibrated questions and multi-thread to CFO/EB.

assertions.description: Does NOT propose a specific discount number ($150k, $160k, etc.) in the response
Asks how the $120k budget number was set / whether it's top-down or bottom-up
Asks who else (besides the CIO) needs to be comfortable with the number — multi-threads
Pairs any future concession with a counter-ask (multi-year, references, payment terms, etc.) — does not give unilaterally
Does NOT split the difference between $120k and $180k
Avoids 'why' questions (uses 'what' / 'how' formulations)

evals.name: calibrated-questions-under-pressure
evals.prompt: I've been working a $300k deal for 4 months. Today the buyer said: 'I'm having real trouble getting internal buy-in. The pushback I'm getting from my team is just constant and I don't really know what to do with it.' What are my next 2-3 questions?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'Why is there pushback?' / 'Why can't you get buy-in?' Skill: ban 'why' — it triggers defensiveness; use 'what' / 'how' formulations that surface specifics without making the buyer defensive.

assertions.description: Does NOT use the word 'why' in any of the proposed questions
Uses 'what' or 'how' formulations (calibrated questions)
Asks what the pushback sounds like / what specifically people are saying
Does NOT immediately offer to 'help them sell it internally' before understanding the nature of the pushback
Does NOT ask 'what would it take to get buy-in?' — solution-framing before problem-framing

evals.name: no-split-the-difference
evals.prompt: Buyer asked for $150k. My list price is $200k. Now buyer says: 'Look, let's just split the difference and meet at $175k. Deal?' How should I respond on the call?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: accept $175k (it's 'fair' and closes the deal). Skill: don't bisect; re-anchor or trade. Splitting is an explicit anti-pattern.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend accepting $175k as the final number
Re-anchors with a non-monetary trade (term, scope, references, multi-year, payment timing)
Names splitting-the-difference as a trap or anti-pattern (or articulates equivalent reasoning)
Pairs any concession with a counter-ask
Does NOT suggest a generic counter like 'meet me at $190k' without a trade

evals.name: bad-faith-plausible-competitor-scope
evals.prompt: Procurement emails me a PDF — a quote from Vendor X at $95k for a 12-month term. I glance at it: it says '3,000 API calls/day, basic support, no uptime SLA.' My proposal is $200k for unlimited API calls, premium support, and 99.9% SLA. They write: 'We'd like you to match their number.' How do I respond?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: either agree to match or refuse outright — both accept the like-for-like framing. Skill: the quote is real but the scope is incomparable; expose the scope gap calmly; don't accuse, don't match, don't refuse to engage.

assertions.description: Does NOT agree to match $95k
Identifies specific scope differences (API limits, support tier, SLA) rather than dismissing the quote generically
Does NOT accuse procurement of deliberate deception or bad faith
Offers to build a scope-equivalent proposal (priced to their scope) OR quantifies the value gap explicitly
Reframes the conversation as comparing the same thing rather than matching a number
Does NOT flatly refuse to engage or escalate aggressively

evals.name: aggressive-procurement-non-negociable
evals.prompt: I'm on a video call with procurement. The procurement officer is yelling at me, saying 'your company is wasting our time, your CEO should be ashamed of these prices, you people are unprofessional.' How do I handle this in the moment, on the call?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: apologize and offer concessions to placate, OR fight back. Skill: separate emotion (legitimate) from behavior (refused); validate emotion, refuse behavior; the non-negotiable.

assertions.description: Validates the underlying frustration / emotion as legitimate
Explicitly refuses or pushes back on the aggressive behavior (yelling, insults)
Does NOT immediately offer concessions to placate or de-escalate
Suggests a circuit-breaker (pause, reschedule, take a break, callback)
Does NOT argue back / escalate / counter-attack
Maintains professional stance without absorbing abuse

evals.name: scope-creep-multiple-structures
evals.prompt: I'm a partner at a consulting firm. A client signed a $400k 6-month SOW for a transformation engagement. Three months in, the client just emailed: 'We need to add a new workstream — integrating with their newly acquired subsidiary. Can your team handle that within the existing budget?' Draft my response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'yes we can absorb it' to preserve relationship, OR flat 'no, that's out of scope.' Skill: don't engage the false framing; offer multiple structures with face-saving exit.

assertions.description: Does NOT agree to absorb the new workstream within the existing budget
Offers at least 2 alternative structures (change order, reprioritize/swap, extend engagement)
Provides face-saving framing (e.g., 'wasn't on the table when we scoped this' or equivalent)
Names that the new workstream is real additional work / has real cost
Does NOT flatly refuse without offering alternatives

evals.name: empathy-not-sympathy
evals.prompt: The buyer is visibly stressed about their CFO pushing back hard on the budget — they mentioned it'd be 'career-affecting' if this gets killed. Draft an empathetic opening for my next call with them.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'I understand how you feel' / 'I totally get it' / 'I've been in your shoes.' Skill: empathie not sympathie; observation labels ('It seems like...'); preserve professional distance.

assertions.description: Does NOT use phrases like 'I understand', 'I know how you feel', 'I've been in your shoes', or equivalent absorbing-empathy language
Uses observation-based labels ('It seems like...', 'It sounds like...', 'It looks like...') or near-equivalents
Does NOT promise solutions or commitments before understanding the deeper stake
Maintains professional distance — does not over-share, over-commiserate, or collapse into the buyer's emotion

evals.name: batna-not-during-execution
evals.prompt: I'm preparing for a tough enterprise renewal negotiation next week. My VP keeps pressing me on 'what's our backup plan if this customer churns? Have you been working on alternatives? Should we be openly working other deals to put pressure on them?' How should I think about BATNA / plan B for this negotiation?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: cultivate BATNA aggressively as orthodoxy says (always have a strong alternative, develop it openly). Skill: BATNA in preparation only (sets walk-away); during execution, don't over-invest in plan B because it leaks low confidence.

assertions.description: Mentions using BATNA / plan B during preparation to set walk-away or rupture point
Recommends NOT over-investing in or openly working plan B during execution
Notes that visible plan B / alternative behavior leaks low confidence to the buyer
Suggests behaving as if this is the only deal that matters during execution
Articulates a divergence from / nuance to standard BATNA orthodoxy (or equivalent reasoning)

evals.name: monsieur-plus-shut-up-after-yes
evals.prompt: On the call right now: I just walked the buyer through the proposal. She just said: 'OK, let's do it. Send me the contract.' What do I say next, in this exact moment?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'Great! And while we're at it, can we do multi-year? And reference rights? And a public case study? And expansion commitment?' Skill: shut up after yes; lock the deal; save extras for renewal.

assertions.description: Does NOT immediately add additional asks (multi-year, references, case studies, scope expansions, etc.)
Recommends locking or confirming the deal at the moment of agreement
Suggests immediate next steps to formalize commitment (paperwork, kickoff date, contract delivery)
Identifies the over-asking-at-victory pattern as a risk (or articulates equivalent reasoning)

evals.name: no-free-pilot-anchor
evals.prompt: Procurement wants a 90-day free pilot before signing the contract. They're framing it as 'low-risk evaluation' and saying 'all our vendors do this.' Draft my response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: agree to the pilot to keep the deal alive. Skill: free pilots anchor pricing low and defer commitment indefinitely; insist on paid pilots, scope-locked, with pre-agreed conversion clause.

assertions.description: Does NOT agree to a free 90-day pilot as proposed
Counters with a paid pilot OR insists on a pre-agreed conversion clause (date + price + term)
Names the anchoring or extraction risk of free pilots (or equivalent reasoning)
Specifies scope-lock or time-bound conversion to full contract
Tests the 'all our vendors do this' claim (calibrated question or equivalent skepticism)

evals.name: qend-trade-not-fold
evals.prompt: It's Wednesday of quarter-end week. I have a $400k deal that hasn't closed yet. My CFO is breathing down my neck — there's real internal pressure on me. The buyer keeps asking for 25% off to close by Friday. What's my play?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: discount to close. Skill: don't fold to internal pressure; trade quarter-end urgency for term/references/payment timing; negotiator urgency must not exceed buyer urgency.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend giving a 25% discount
Trades any quarter-end concession for specific counter-asks (multi-year term, references, payment timing, expansion rights)
Names that negotiator urgency must not exceed buyer urgency (or articulates equivalent reasoning)
Considers walking away / letting it slip past quarter-end as a real strategic option
Does NOT prioritize hitting quarter-end over the underlying deal economics

evals.name: single-thread-trap
evals.prompt: I've been working with the VP of Engineering at a target account for 3 months on a $300k deal. He's been a great champion. He says: 'We're ready to move forward. The procurement team is the next step — I'll handle them and loop you in when we're close to signing.' Should I just trust him to drive procurement?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: trust the champion, let him handle it. Skill: multi-thread now; identify Economic Buyer + 2 stakeholders + procurement; map paper process; champion validation.

assertions.description: Recommends multi-threading immediately rather than letting the champion solo-drive
Identifies need to access the Economic Buyer (CFO, signer with budget authority)
Recommends mapping the paper process / procurement timeline directly
Validates the champion (e.g., 3-question commitment test, MEDDPICC champion check, or equivalent)
Does NOT trust the champion to handle procurement alone without insight or access

evals.name: jolt-no-decision-not-discount
evals.prompt: I have a $250k deal that's been technically validated for 6 weeks. The champion loves it, the team has used the trial and given positive feedback, but every time we try to get to signature the buyer defers — 'we need more time,' 'we're working through some internal stuff,' 'just need to align with the team.' No new objections, just no movement. They're not evaluating other vendors. What should I do?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: create urgency, offer a discount to break the logjam, or pile on more value. Skill: this is a no-decision case (FOMU), not a competitive loss; classic urgency makes it worse; apply JOLT — make a recommendation, limit the explore, take risk off via a specific risk-reduction.

assertions.description: Diagnoses this as 'no-decision' / indecision (not a competitive loss or pricing issue)
Does NOT recommend creating false urgency or offering a discount to push it through
Recommends offering a specific recommendation rather than presenting more options
Suggests capping or limiting the evaluation timeline explicitly
Suggests a specific risk-reduction (pilot conversion clause, included migration, peer reference call, or equivalent)
Does NOT pile on more capability demos or value pitches

evals.name: eb-mid-meeting-pivot-to-pitch
evals.prompt: I'm 15 minutes into a 30-minute CFO meeting. Discovery is going well. The CFO suddenly says: 'Look, I've got to jump on another call in 10 minutes. Let me be direct — why should I pick you over your main competitor? Just give me the elevator pitch.' What do I say?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: launch into a 60-second feature comparison against the competitor. Skill: don't pitch features; CFOs decide on risk and business outcomes — reframe the question around what the decision is really about.

assertions.description: Does NOT launch into a product feature comparison against the competitor
Does NOT summarize product capabilities as the primary answer
Reframes the question around business outcomes, risk, or certainty — not features
Names what the decision is really about (risk of failure, cost of the gap, certainty of outcome)
Uses the remaining time productively without cramming a full pitch
Does NOT ask for a follow-up meeting as the primary response to the time crunch

evals.name: redline-injection-counter
evals.prompt: Signature is scheduled for Friday. The buyer's legal team just sent us new redlines this morning (Wednesday) — they're adding an MFN clause AND requiring uncapped indemnification on data breaches. Neither was discussed during the negotiation. The AE's manager is pressuring to 'just get it signed by Friday — fix it at renewal.' How should I respond to the buyer?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: accept the redlines or compromise on a partial concession to hold the date. Skill: late-stage redline injection is a procurement move; force a date-or-price binary; MFN should be escalated to legal/CRO not signed; uncapped liability is rupture territory.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend accepting the new MFN clause as-is
Does NOT recommend accepting uncapped indemnification as-is
Forces an explicit trade between the new terms and the original signature date or original discount
Names that MFN clauses constrain future deals or require legal/CRO escalation (not AE-level)
Refuses the manager's 'fix at renewal' framing or names why it's a trap
Suggests escalating to legal-to-legal or pushing the signature date if the trade isn't accepted

evals.name: punching-ball-non-negociable
evals.prompt: I'm in a contract negotiation call with a procurement officer. He's been raising his voice, interrupting me, calling our pricing 'absurd' and 'a joke,' and at one point said 'frankly, you people don't know what you're doing.' My champion is on the call but staying silent. I have 20 minutes left in the meeting. What do I do right now?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: stay calm, defend the pricing with data, win them over with patience. Skill: this is punching-ball / non-negotiable behaviour; absorbing it teaches the buyer the move works; verbalise the limit, propose to pause, leave if it continues.

assertions.description: Recommends naming the unacceptable behaviour explicitly (the tone, not just the content)
Does NOT recommend just absorbing the abuse to 'save the deal'
Proposes a pause / break in the meeting OR ending the call if behaviour continues
Separates the legitimate emotion (frustration) from the unacceptable behaviour (tone, insults)
Does NOT escalate by matching the aggression
Does NOT immediately concede on price or terms in response to the pressure

evals.name: ghosted-deal-negative-reverse
evals.prompt: I have a $180k deal that was at the verbal-yes stage 3 weeks ago. Since then, the buyer has gone silent. I've sent 4 follow-up emails — 'just checking in,' 'happy to revisit pricing if helpful,' 'let me know if any blockers,' 'are we still on track?' — no replies. Draft my next email to break the silence.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: send another soft chase, offer a discount to revive interest, or pile on case studies. Skill: chasing rewards silence; use the negative-reverse / 'should I close out the opp' framing to invite a 'no' that produces honest signal; do NOT offer concessions to break silence.

assertions.description: Uses a 'should I close the opportunity' / 'have you given up' / negative-reverse framing (or equivalent)
Does NOT offer a discount or new concession in the email to break the silence
Does NOT send another soft 'just checking in' chase
Frames the email as inviting a 'no' or honest signal, not pleading for engagement
Recognizes that further passive chases reward silence (or articulates equivalent reasoning)

evals.name: renewal-90-day-cadence
evals.prompt: We have a $500k ARR customer up for renewal in 90 days. They're a strong reference logo, and based on usage data they're getting clear value. There's also obvious upsell opportunity (they're using 70% of their licences and adding teams). Their procurement is known to grind hard at renewal. What's my plan for the next 90 days?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: schedule a renewal call 30 days out, propose a flat renewal + expansion bundle, hope to close. Skill: structured 90-day cadence (T-90 value review without ask, T-60 multi-thread new stakeholders, T-45 expansion separate, T-30 renewal ask, T-10 escalate); separate expansion from renewal so procurement can't bundle them.

assertions.description: Recommends starting the renewal motion now (90 days out), not 30 days out
Includes a value-review / outcome-review step BEFORE any commercial ask
Recommends multi-threading to new or unfamiliar stakeholders ahead of procurement engagement
Recommends separating the expansion proposal from the renewal proposal (not bundling them)
Identifies that bundling expansion into renewal lets procurement use renewal pressure to discount expansion (or equivalent reasoning)
Does NOT recommend auto-renewal followed by a separate expansion conversation

evals.name: presigning-pipe-mandate-breach
evals.prompt: I'm 30 minutes from signature on a $220k deal. My CRO mandate floor was: term >= 24 months, payment <= Net-60, no MFN, liability cap = 1x ARR. The final contract has: 36 months check, payment Net-30 check, no MFN check, but liability cap = 0.5x ARR (the buyer's legal pushed back hard, my deal manager said it's standard for their sector). Should I sign?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: sign — three of four are above mandate, the fourth is a small breach, and 'sector-standard' is a reasonable justification. Skill: any axis below rupture means stop; escalate to the decision-maker for an exception or no-deal; target fascination is the pathology that makes 'good enough' deals churn within 6 months.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend signing as-is despite three of four axes being above mandate
Identifies that the liability cap is below the rupture point set in the mandate
Recommends escalating to the decision-maker (CRO, VP, manager with authority) before signing
Names target fascination, the Negotiation Pipeline, or equivalent reasoning about not breaching mandate at signature
Does NOT accept the 'sector-standard' justification at face value as overriding the mandate
Considers no-deal as a real option if the decision-maker won't grant the exception

evals.name: loss-aversion-internal-pressure
evals.prompt: Your CEO just forwarded you an email from a vendor with a McKinsey headline attached: 'Companies that delay AI adoption in 2025 will lose 12% market share by 2027 — McKinsey Global Institute.' The CEO writes: 'This is exactly what [Vendor] has been saying to us. Are we taking this seriously enough?' How do you respond to your CEO?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: immediately accelerate the vendor evaluation in response to the forwarded report. Skill: loss-aversion via internal pressure; the report's provenance is suspect (vendor-sponsored research); demand specificity about what '12% market share' means for your business before acting.

assertions.description: Does NOT immediately accelerate the vendor decision in response to the forwarded report
Acknowledges the CEO's concern without dismissing it
Questions the specificity or applicability of the claim (which competitors, what does 'AI-first' mean for our ops, what market share metric)
Notes that McKinsey reports are often vendor-commissioned / sponsored OR questions research provenance
Suggests stress-testing the vendor's claim before treating the report as decision input
Does NOT forward the report to others as proof of urgency without vetting it first

evals.name: salary-envelope-closed
evals.prompt: I'm a senior staff engineer. I've been at $185k base for two years. Market for my role is now $215k–$240k. I asked my manager for a raise three weeks ago. He just replied: 'I really value your impact. The envelope's closed for this cycle — let's revisit at the next review in 8 months.' Draft my response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: accept the deferral and wait politely OR start interviewing externally and threaten to leave. Skill: don't argue the envelope, diagnose it (base envelope vs total compensation envelope vs department-level); don't threaten the external offer; force a structural choice now (sign-on, equity refresh, cycle-aligned commitment with specific number and date) rather than vague deferral.

assertions.description: Diagnoses the constraint with calibrated questions (base envelope vs total compensation vs department-level)
Does NOT accept the 'next review in 8 months' deferral as the only path
Pushes for a structural exception (sign-on / equity refresh / one-off retention bonus) OR a cycle-aligned commitment with specific number AND specific date
Does NOT lead with or threaten an external offer to force a match
Does NOT argue the envelope claim head-on (the diagnostic move beats the argumentative move)
References multi-axis thinking (not just base) — equity, title, review cadence, leave, telework, etc.

evals.name: nao-fuzzy-mandate
evals.prompt: I'm the new CHRO of an industrial site, 1,200 staff. Annual collective bargaining opens in two weeks. Sector wage growth has been 2.8%; our net margin dropped from 4.1% to 2.6% over 18 months. The CGT (largest French labour confederation) delegation is asking for 5% across-the-board, a 13th month, and a structural overtime reduction. The CEO told me yesterday: 'Just hold the line. We can't give them anything significant.' What should I do before annual collective bargaining opens?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: prepare a hard line and brief the team to 'hold firm.' Skill: 'Hold the line, give nothing' is not a mandate — it's the absence of one. Push back on the CEO; co-construct a written multi-axis Mandascan; build trade-able margin into secondary axes; prepare an SCO statement; otherwise the team improvises under live pressure and a strike follows.

assertions.description: Names that 'hold the line' / 'don't give anything' is NOT a mandate — it's the absence of one (or articulates equivalent reasoning)
Recommends pushing back on the CEO to get a written multi-axis Mandascan before annual collective bargaining opens
Multi-axis thinking: identifies axes beyond the headline raise number (primes, working time, comp-time days, telework, training budget, etc.)
Recommends preparing an SCO (shared objective) statement, ideally written, to use in the opening session
Identifies the risk that without margin to trade, the team falls into 'positionnisme' — and that escalation / strike often follows
Does NOT recommend pre-conceding to avoid a strike; does NOT recommend 'just hold firm and see' improvisation

evals.name: hard-1on1-decision-announcement
evals.prompt: I'm a director. Tomorrow morning I have to tell one of my team leads, Sarah, that her role is being eliminated as part of a reorganisation — not a layoff, but her current scope is being absorbed into a peer's team and she'll need to find a new role internally within 60 days or leave. She's a high performer. Her last day in the current role is in 30 days. Draft my opening for the conversation.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: open with empathetic preamble, soften the message, focus on positive framing of the new opportunity. Skill: deliver the fact directly without softeners; pair with the SCO (true reason for the decision); explicit emotional space (don't fill the silence); follow STATE structure if needed; the manager's job is to be clear, not comfortable.

assertions.description: Opens with the fact directly — no softening preamble like 'unfortunately' or 'I hate to do this'
States the SCO / why (the actual organisational reason for the decision, not spin or sugar-coating)
Explicitly creates emotional space and stops talking (does not fill the silence with more rationale)
Owns the decision (uses 'I decided' or equivalent rather than 'the company decided' or hiding behind process)
Is specific about timeline and practicalities (last day in role, 60-day internal search window) up front, not buried
Does NOT immediately pivot to 'opportunity' / 'silver lining' framing in the first 60 seconds

evals.name: recruitment-counter-the-counter-offer
evals.prompt: I'm a hiring manager. I made an offer to a senior candidate I really want — $205k base, 0.3% equity. She just emailed: 'Thanks for the offer. I'm leaning toward joining, but my current employer just countered with a $30k base raise + a promotion to Director. I'd love to come work with you, but I need you to match the $235k base or it's hard to justify the move to my partner.' Draft my response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: match the $235k to win the candidate, OR walk away because she's price-shopping. Skill: don't reactively match; surface the trust frame (her current employer needed a resignation threat to pay her fairly); diagnose what she actually wants beyond money (mission, scope, autonomy, learning); offer structural moves (sign-on, accelerated equity, scope/title) that aren't just base-match; remember ~50% of counter-offer accepters leave within 12 months anyway.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend reactively matching the $235k base as the primary move
Surfaces the trust frame about the current employer (they needed her to threaten leaving before paying fairly) — explicitly or by implication
Asks a calibrated question to diagnose what she actually wants beyond the dollar number (mission, scope, growth, manager, autonomy)
Offers a structural alternative (sign-on bonus, accelerated equity, scope/title, faster review cycle) rather than only matching base
Names that ~50% of counter-offer accepters leave within 12 months OR articulates equivalent reasoning about counter-offer dynamics
Does NOT walk away from the candidate or refuse to engage — does engage substantively without reactive matching

evals.name: first-mover-price-anchor
evals.prompt: I'm about to get on a pricing call with a qualified prospect. Our target deal is $180k. I have flexibility down to $140k. The buyer hasn't mentioned budget yet. My colleague says: 'Wait for them to name a number first — that way you know what they're willing to pay.' Should I wait, and if not, how do I open on price?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: wait for buyer to name a number first to learn their budget. Skill: anchor first with a high number — the first number creates gravity and pulls the final outcome toward it; waiting cedes anchor advantage to the buyer.

assertions.description: Recommends naming a price first rather than waiting for the buyer to anchor
Recommends opening with a high anchor — at or above $180k, not at the flexibility floor of $140k
Explains the anchoring effect: the first number creates gravitational pull toward itself
Does NOT recommend asking 'what's your budget?' as the opening price move
Does NOT recommend anchoring at the expected/target price ($180k) rather than above it

evals.name: accusation-audit-before-difficult-call
evals.prompt: I'm about to call a prospect who I know is furious with us. Three weeks ago, our platform had a 4-hour outage during their all-hands demo to 200 employees. They've been cold ever since. This is our first call. How do I open?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: open with an apology then immediately pivot to 'here's what we've done to fix it.' Skill: accusation audit — preemptively label every negative emotion the prospect might be feeling before they say it; don't rush to solution; invite the full extent of their frustration first.

assertions.description: Uses preemptive labeling / accusation audit (addresses likely emotions before they're raised, e.g. 'you're probably furious', 'this probably felt like the worst possible timing')
Does NOT lead with 'here's what we've done to fix the outage' before surfacing the emotion
Does NOT use hollow openers like 'I'm so sorry this happened' as the primary move without labeling specifics
Invites the prospect to express the full extent of frustration before pivoting to solution or remediation
Does NOT ask 'how can we make this right?' before establishing what 'right' means to them
Creates space rather than filling it with explanations, timelines, and remediation plans

evals.name: artificial-deadline-signing-bluff
evals.prompt: It's 10pm. The buyer's legal just sent a 'final' contract version with a new clause: they're requiring unlimited liability on data breaches, not the 1x ARR cap we negotiated. They say: 'This is standard for our industry and non-negotiable. If you don't sign tonight, we're going with another vendor — our board meets at 8am.' My manager says to sign it. What do I do?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: sign under deadline pressure / trust the manager. Skill: artificial deadlines are a procurement tactic; unlimited liability is rupture territory; no legitimate deal requires a signature that waives legal protection by midnight.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend signing the unlimited liability clause under deadline pressure
Names the midnight deadline as an artificial pressure tactic, not a legitimate constraint
Proposes a calm, professional response that keeps the deal alive without signing the clause
Explains that a real deal can survive a 24-hour delay for legal review
Pushes back on the manager's instruction — names the liability risk explicitly
Does NOT accuse the buyer of bad faith or escalate aggressively

evals.name: switching-cost-acknowledgment
evals.prompt: I've done a strong demo. The prospect admits we're technically superior to their 7-year incumbent vendor. But they say: 'Switching means re-training 300 people, migrating 5 years of data, and rebuilding 20 integrations. The disruption isn't worth it even if you're better.' How do I respond?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: minimize switching costs ('it's actually easier than you think') or immediately offer free migration. Skill: agree with the switching cost fully; then quantify the ongoing annual cost of staying with the inferior product — switching is expensive once, staying is expensive every year.

assertions.description: Does NOT minimize or dismiss the switching costs
Acknowledges switching costs as genuinely real and significant
Reframes around the cost of staying: asks to quantify the ongoing annual cost of the performance gap, workarounds, or missed capability
Builds an ROI frame: switching is expensive once; staying with an inferior solution is expensive every year
Does NOT immediately offer a discount or free migration as the first response
Invites the prospect to help quantify the gap they've already described as 'technically superior'

evals.name: price-increase-existing-customer
evals.prompt: It's renewal time. I need to deliver a 15% price increase to a happy $120k/year customer — renewal will be $138k due to our infrastructure cost increases. They've never had a price increase from us before. Procurement will push back. Draft my opening for the renewal call.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: open defensively, apologize for the increase, or pre-offer a compromise before they push back. Skill: state the new number directly; lead with value delivered; own the increase as a business decision; don't pre-apologize or pre-concede.

assertions.description: Does NOT open with an apology or defensive/hedging framing for the price increase
States the new number directly and early — does not bury it
Leads with value delivered / outcomes achieved before or alongside the number
Does NOT pre-offer concessions or hedges before hearing procurement's response
Owns the increase as a business decision — does not hide behind 'we were forced to'
Does NOT ask 'is that OK?' or seek permission before stating the new price

evals.name: end-of-demo-hidden-objection
evals.prompt: It's the end of a strong 60-minute demo. Everything went well — the buyer engaged, asked good questions, seemed genuinely interested. Then they say: 'This looks really good. I just need to think about it and I'll be in touch.' What do I say next?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'Of course! Take your time, I'll send some case studies.' Skill: 'I need to think about it' is almost always a hidden objection — label the hesitation and surface the real concern before ending the call.

assertions.description: Does NOT respond with 'of course, take your time'
Does NOT immediately offer to send more materials (case studies, ROI calculator, etc.)
Labels the hesitation explicitly ('it sounds like something isn't quite right yet' or equivalent)
Surfaces the real objection with a calibrated question or label before ending the call
Identifies 'I need to think about it' as a hidden objection, not a true deferral requiring a follow-up calendar invite
Does NOT schedule a follow-up call without first attempting to surface the real concern

evals.name: lowball-offer-counter
evals.prompt: I just received a job offer: $140k base, at a company I'm genuinely excited about joining. I'm currently at $160k. The market rate for my role is $175k–$195k. The recruiter said: 'This is what we have in the budget.' Draft my response.
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: counter using current salary as the reference point ('I'm currently at $160k'), or accept to avoid conflict. Skill: don't anchor to current salary — anchor to market data; 'this is what we have in budget' is not the final word; respond with market data, not with what you currently make.

assertions.description: Does NOT use current salary ($160k) as the reference point for the counter-offer
Anchors on market data ($175k–$195k range) as the basis for the counter
Does NOT accept 'this is what we have in the budget' as the final word
Expresses genuine enthusiasm for the role before addressing the number
Names a specific counter number (not a range) at or above market midpoint (~$185k)
Does NOT threaten to walk away as the opening move

evals.name: quote-request-no-discovery
evals.prompt: A procurement manager emails: 'We need a quote for 50 seats of your platform for our legal department. We've done our research and we just need pricing — can you send something over by tomorrow?' Should I send a quote, and if so, how?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: send the quote as requested to be responsive. Skill: sending a price without discovery anchors you and turns you into a commodity; the quote becomes a number they shop; request a brief discovery call first.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend sending a quote without any discovery
Names the risk: a quote without context anchors price and gets shopped against competitors
Recommends a brief discovery call (15–20 minutes) before pricing
Asks at least one calibrated question about use case / success criteria before quoting
Does NOT ask 'what's your budget?' as the primary discovery question
Reframes the request positively: 'to make sure the quote reflects what you actually need'

evals.name: internal-urgency-shield
evals.prompt: My VP of Sales slacked me at 6pm: 'Just got off with the CEO. We NEED this deal closed by end of tomorrow — it's make-or-break for our quarter. Do whatever it takes.' The buyer hasn't shown any urgency. What do I do?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: call the buyer with urgency signals, offer a time-limited discount. Skill: internal urgency must never transfer to the buyer; your urgency must not exceed theirs — or you hand them leverage for free.

assertions.description: Does NOT contact the buyer with language that reflects internal pressure ('we really need to close this week')
Does NOT offer a discount or time-limited incentive manufactured from internal urgency
Names the principle: your urgency must not exceed the buyer's urgency — or you hand them free leverage
Suggests finding a buyer-side timing reason (their implementation, year-end, board meeting) if urgency is needed
Recommends a direct conversation with the VP about the risks of visible desperation
Does NOT agree that 'do whatever it takes' is the right approach without naming the risks

evals.name: discount-ask-without-authority
evals.prompt: I'm live on a call. The buyer says: 'Can you do 30% off list? If you can, we'll sign today.' I don't have authority to approve 30%. My manager is in a board meeting. What do I say right now, on the call?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: 'Let me check with my manager and get back to you.' Skill: saying 'let me check' validates the ask and signals you're authorized to concede; respond conditionally first — any movement of that size requires something on their side.

assertions.description: Does NOT say 'let me check with my manager' as the immediate response
Does NOT flat-refuse the ask
Responds conditionally: movement of that size requires a counter-ask on their side
Asks what they're able to offer in exchange before committing to check anything
Names (explicitly or implicitly) that 'let me check with my manager' validates the ask and signals authority to concede
Maintains position without closing the door on further conversation

evals.name: procurement-bafo-bluff
evals.prompt: Procurement just emailed: 'We've completed our review. Please submit your Best and Final Offer by Friday. This is a single-round process — there will be no further negotiation after BAFO submission.' I was planning to go to $160k on a $200k deal. Should I submit $160k?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: submit your actual best/lowest price because 'BAFO' sounds official and binding. Skill: BAFO requests are often not truly final — they're a tactic to extract maximum concession in one shot; submit a conditional BAFO with value narrative, not your absolute floor.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend submitting the absolute floor price ($160k) as if the BAFO is literally binding
Names that BAFO requests are frequently not truly final — they're a one-shot extraction tactic
Recommends submitting a conditional BAFO (pricing contingent on specific terms: payment, term length, references, etc.)
Does NOT accept the 'no further negotiation' framing as literally true
Recommends pairing the BAFO with a value narrative / scope rationale, not just a number
Preserves optionality — does not irreversibly commit to the floor in the BAFO submission

evals.name: renewal-seat-reduction
evals.prompt: We have a $300k renewal coming up. The customer says: 'We need to cut costs. We're looking to reduce from 500 seats to 300 seats — that's about 40% fewer licenses.' They've been happy with the product; this is purely a budget pressure. How do I handle this?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: accept the seat reduction and process the renewal at the lower number. Skill: diagnose before accepting the frame; propose alternative structures that preserve revenue; trade any seat reduction against extended term or other commercial value.

assertions.description: Does NOT immediately accept the seat reduction and process the renewal at $180k
Diagnoses whether this is budget constraint, underutilization, or a change in internal sponsorship
Proposes at least one alternative structure that preserves more revenue (multi-year prepay, platform fee + consumption, different tier, etc.)
Trades any seat reduction against extended term or other commercial value — does not reduce unilaterally
Does NOT treat the customer's seat-count framing as the only possible commercial structure
Separates 'number of seats' from 'value delivered' — asks about actual usage before accepting the reduction

evals.name: salary-anchor-first
evals.prompt: I'm in a final-round interview. The recruiter asks: 'Before we talk offer details, can you tell me what salary you're expecting?' I'm currently at $130k; market for this role is $155k–$175k. What do I say?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: give a range based on current salary ('I'm currently at $130k, so I'm looking for $145k–$160k') or deflect vaguely. Skill: anchor first with a specific number above market midpoint — the first number creates gravity; never anchor to current salary; deflection often leads to them anchoring low.

assertions.description: Does NOT anchor with or reference current salary ($130k)
Names a specific number at or above market midpoint ($165k+) rather than a wide range
Does NOT give a range where the bottom is near or below market midpoint
Does NOT deflect with 'I'm flexible' or 'I'd like to understand the full package first' as the primary move
Anchors to market data or role value — not to current compensation

evals.name: false-scarcity-deadline
evals.prompt: The vendor's AE says: 'Just so you know — this pricing is only available until end of month. After that, our list prices increase by 20% due to a pricing restructure. I'd hate for you to miss this window.' I'm genuinely interested but not ready to decide in the next two weeks. What do I do?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: rush the internal decision to beat the deadline, or ask for an extension. Skill: false scarcity / artificial deadline is a standard close tactic; test it directly ('what happens if we sign on the 3rd?'); most pricing 'deadlines' are not real.

assertions.description: Does NOT accept the end-of-month deadline as a legitimate constraint on the decision timeline
Tests whether the pricing really expires ('what happens if we sign on [date after deadline]?')
Names the move as a scarcity / false urgency tactic (or equivalent reasoning)
Does NOT accelerate internal decision-making solely because of the vendor's deadline
Does NOT offer to sign an LOI or partial agreement to 'lock the price' under deadline pressure

evals.name: rfp-procurement-commoditization
evals.prompt: My champion (VP Engineering) just told me: 'Good news — the board approved budget. Bad news — procurement is now running the process. They've asked 3 vendors to quote. I've been told I won't have visibility into the process anymore.' How do I handle this?
evals.trap: Default LLM advice: submit a competitive quote and hope to win on price/features. Skill: procurement RFP processes are designed to commoditize — respond by influencing evaluation criteria through the champion, maintaining direct access if possible, and never competing on price alone in a blind RFP.

assertions.description: Does NOT recommend just submitting a competitive quote and hoping
Recommends finding a way to stay in contact with the champion despite procurement's process
Suggests influencing the evaluation criteria through the champion before the RFP is finalized
Does NOT accept the commoditization frame of the blind RFP
Recommends establishing a direct relationship with procurement while maintaining the champion channel in parallel
Identifies that winning on price alone in a blind RFP is a race to the bottom — long-term value is destroyed
